content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Part 4 of the “Frontend Performance That Users Feel” series. Three metrics matter. Most of the dashboard is noise.
Open any performance tool and you’re buried in numbers: TTFB, FCP, TBT, Speed Index, TTI, and a dozen more acronyms. Teams either ignore all of them or, worse, optimize a vanity metric that doesn’t change anything a user feels. Meanwhile, Google has quietly told everyone exactly which metrics matter — they’re the Core Web Vitals, they affect your search ranking, and they correlate with real outcomes like bounce rate and conversions.
There are only three. Understanding what each one measures — and the single biggest fix for each — is most of the performance battle. Let me cut through the noise.
A fast page isn’t one with good lab scores. It’s one where content appears quickly (LCP), responds instantly when tapped (INP), and doesn’t jump around while you use it (CLS). Those three are what users actually feel.
The Three That Matter
Metric Measures Good
LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) How fast the main content appears < 2.5s
INP (Interaction to Next Paint) How fast the page responds to input < 200ms
CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift) How much the layout jumps around < 0.1Each maps to a distinct human frustration: waiting for content, tapping and nothing happens, and reaching for a button that just moved. Fix these three and the page feels fast, regardless of what the other twenty metrics say.
LCP: Make the Main Content Appear Fast
LCP measures when the largest visible element — usually a hero image, heading, or banner — finishes rendering. It’s your “is anything here yet?” metric.
Biggest fixes:

• Optimize the LCP image (Part 3) — right format, right size, and critically, don’t lazy-load it. Preload it if needed: <link rel="preload" as="image" href="hero.avif">.

• Cut render-blocking resources — defer non-critical CSS/JS so the browser can paint the main content sooner.

• Improve server response time (TTFB) — a slow backend delays everything downstream.
LCP is mostly about getting the one biggest thing on screen quickly and not letting scripts or slow servers block it.
INP: Make Interactions Respond Instantly
INP (which replaced the old FID) measures the delay between a user action — tap, click, keypress — and the screen visibly updating. High INP feels like a frozen, unresponsive page.
The usual culprit is long JavaScript tasks blocking the main thread. When a tap fires but the thread is busy running a 300 ms script, the response is stuck behind it.
Biggest fixes:

• Break up long tasks — split heavy work so the browser can respond to input between chunks (yield to the main thread, requestIdleCallback, or chunked processing).

• Reduce the re-render storm (Part 2) — a tap that triggers 50 component re-renders is a slow interaction.

• Move heavy computation off the main thread with a Web Worker.
INP is the metric your single-page app most likely fails, because SPAs do a lot of work on the main thread.
CLS: Stop the Layout From Jumping
CLS measures unexpected layout shifts — content moving after it first appeared, usually because something loaded late and pushed everything down. It’s why you tap “Cancel” and hit “Confirm” because an ad loaded a half-second earlier.
Biggest fixes:

• Set dimensions on images and video (Part 3) — width/height so space is reserved.

• Reserve space for dynamic content — ads, embeds, banners: give them a fixed box before they load.

• Avoid inserting content above existing content — don’t push the page down when a late element arrives.

• Use font-display: optional/swap carefully so font loading doesn't reflow text.
CLS is the cheapest of the three to fix and the most jarring to users when ignored.
Fix: Measure Real Users, Not Just Your Laptop
Lab tools (Lighthouse) are great for debugging, but your laptop isn’t your user. The scores that affect ranking come from field data — real users on real devices:

• Use the Chrome User Experience Report (CrUX) or a Real User Monitoring (RUM) tool to see your actual LCP/INP/CLS distribution.

• Optimize for the 75th percentile, not the average — the metric is about your slower users, who are the ones bouncing.
A perfect Lighthouse score on your fast machine means nothing if real users on mid-range phones are seeing 5-second LCPs.
Putting It Together

• LCP — optimize and preload the hero, unblock rendering, speed up the server.

• INP — break up long JS tasks, cut re-render storms, offload heavy work.

• CLS — reserve space for images, ads, and dynamic content.

• Measure real users at the 75th percentile, not your laptop’s average.
The Takeaway
Performance dashboards drown you in metrics, most of which don’t map to anything a user feels or a business measures. Google did the hard work of narrowing it to three: does content show up fast (LCP), does the page respond to touch (INP), and does it hold still while you use it (CLS). These correlate with bounce rate, conversions, and search ranking — so they’re where your effort pays off. Ignore the acronym soup, fix these three for your real users, and you’ll improve the things that actually matter at the same time.
